column1: A. MEMBER PROFILE  (Baseline: Rahul, 28, ?20L CTC — case study specification)

column3: Case study baseline per competition specification. Mid-point of the 23–35 target cohort.
column5: Drives accumulation period = Retirement Age ? Current Age

column3: WeCare plan defined retirement age per case specification; standard in Indian corporate DB plans.
column5: Change to 58 or 65 to test early/late retirement impact

column3: Competition-specified baseline: ?20,00,000 p.a. — mid-level hire in a large domestic corporate.
column5: All contribution values derive from this via Basic/CTC ratio

column3: Industry norm for large Indian corporates: basic = 40–50% of CTC. EPF & WeCare contributions sit on basic.
column5: Lower basic % ? lower EPF/WeCare contributions but higher take-home — key structural lever

column1: Current Monthly Expenses as % of Net Salary
column3: Case specification: 50% of net salary on current expenses — urban lifestyle spend.
column5: Determines disposable income available for voluntary savings (ELSS SIP, extra NPS)
Higher growth ? larger final salary ? harder to hit replacement ratio from fixed-rate vehicles. Note: a flat 8%/yr for 32 straight years is not realistic — salary growth tapers with seniority.

column3: RBI medium-term inflation target band: 4% ± 2%. Set at 6% to capture food & urban cost pressure.
column5: Used to express retirement values in today's purchasing power

column3: Slightly lower than pre-retirement (lower transport/childcare, higher medical). Net 5% assumed.
column5: Affects real value of fixed DB pension over the retirement horizon

column3: Statutory: Section 6, EPF & MP Act 1952 — 12% of basic salary mandatory.
column5: Cannot be reduced below 12%; can voluntarily add more via VPF

column3: Statutory 12% employer share. 8.33% routes to EPS, 3.67% to EPF; full 12% modelled in EPF since EPS pension is small.
column5: EPS provides a small separate pension, not modelled here for simplicity

column3: EPFO declared rate FY 2024-25: 8.15% p.a., sovereign guaranteed. Ranged 8.1%-8.65% over the last decade.
column5: EEE regime: no tax on contribution, growth, or withdrawal after 5 years

column3: Set to 0% in base case. Employee can voluntarily contribute up to 100% of basic at EPF rate.
column5: VPF earns the same 8.15% rate — useful lever for risk-averse savers

column1: D. NPS — NATIONAL PENSION SYSTEM (Tier I)
NPS Employee Voluntary Contribution (% of basic)
column3: Competition default: 5% voluntary. PFRDA allows 0–unlimited; 5% is realistic for early adopters.
column5: Tax deductible under Section 80CCD(1B): additional ?50,000 deduction over the 80C limit

column3: 10% employer NPS contribution is tax-deductible for the employer under Section 80CCD(2).
column5: Most favourable NPS tax treatment — full deduction on employer contribution received by employee

column1: NPS Equity Allocation (% of NPS corpus)
column3: Active Choice: max 75% equity allowed up to age 50; Lifecycle Fund auto-reduces post-50.
column5: Returns weighted: equity_alloc × equity_return + (1?equity_alloc) × debt_return

column3: Long-run Nifty 50 CAGR ? 12–13% (1996–2024). NPS C-scheme historically delivered 10-12%.
column5: Feeds the blended NPS return calculated below

column3: NPS G-scheme (govt securities): 7–8% p.a. historically. 7% assumed conservatively.
column5: Feeds the blended NPS return calculated below

column1: NPS Blended Return (= equity% × equity_ret + debt% × debt_ret)
column3: Weighted average of equity and debt returns based on allocation — used throughout the model for NPS accumulation.
column5: Auto-calculated — do not overwrite

column1: NPS Annuity Compulsion (% of corpus used to buy annuity)
column3: PFRDA regulation: minimum 40% of NPS corpus must purchase an annuity from an IRDAI-registered ASP at age 60.
column5: Remaining 60% is a tax-free lump sum; 40% is the statutory floor

column3: Illustrative rate from LIC Jeevan Akshay VII / HDFC Life annuity products (2024).
column5: Monthly NPS pension = (NPS corpus × 40%) × annuity rate / 12

column3: Illustrative voluntary SIP — ?10,000/month, material but affordable (~9% of take-home at ?20L CTC).
column5: Set to 0 to model an EPF + NPS only scenario

column1: ELSS / Equity MF SIP Annual Step-Up Rate
column3: Assumes SIP increases 10% per year alongside salary growth, maintaining a constant SIP-to-income ratio.
column5: Set to 0 for a flat (non-escalating) SIP

column3: Long-run large-cap equity return in India: 12–14% CAGR (Nifty 50 since 1996). 13% used as base case.
column5: 3-year ELSS lock-in; after lock-in the corpus stays invested

column1: ELSS / MF Expense Ratio (deducted from gross return)
column3: Direct-plan TER for ELSS funds: ~0.5–1.5%. 1% used as mid-point.
column5: Net return used in accumulation = gross return ? expense ratio (?12% net)

column1: ELSS Safe Withdrawal Rate at Retirement
column3: Classic 'safe withdrawal rate' (Bengen 4% Rule, adapted for Indian equity/debt glide path) — the sustainable annual drawdown from an equity corpus without depleting it over a 25-30 year retirement.
column5: Converts the ELSS lump sum into an equivalent monthly income stream for replacement-ratio purposes: Monthly income = ELSS corpus × SWR / 12

column1: G. WECARE DB PLAN — DEFINED BENEFIT PENSION

column3: Fixed per plan specification: 10% of base salary per month until age 60 — this is the employee's cost.
column5: Plan rule, not variable — funds the employer's pension reserve

column1: WeCare Employer Guaranteed Pension (% of final avg salary)
column3: Plan rule: 50% of the employee's average salary over the final 12 months of service — classic DB formula.
column5: This is the employer's guaranteed obligation regardless of market returns

column1: WeCare Commutation % (portion commuted to lump sum at 60)
column3: Up to 1/3 (33%) of pension may be commuted to a lump sum at retirement (cf. 7th Pay Commission: up to 40%).
column5: Commuted amount is tax-free under Section 10(10A); remaining 67% is taxable monthly pension

column1: Commutation Factor (lump sum per ?1 of annual pension)
column3: Derived from a 6% annuity discount rate and the LIC 2020-21 mortality table for age 60, 15-year certain annuity.
column5: Lump sum = commuted annual pension × commutation factor; factor falls as rates rise or age increases

column1: Effective Income Tax Rate on Gross Salary
column3: Indicative effective rate for ?20L CTC: ~20% (old regime, after 80C/HRA/standard deduction). Simplified — consult a CA.
column5: All 80C benefits assumed fully utilised

column1: EPF Admin / EDLI / Other PF Charges (% of basic)
column3: Employer's EPF admin charges: ~0.5% of basic (EDLI + admin) — an employer cost, not deducted from employee pay.
column5: Informational only — not deducted from employee take-home

column1: Gratuity Provision (% of basic, employer cost)
column3: Payment of Gratuity Act 1972: 15/26 × basic/12 per year of service ? 1.54× basic over 32 years ? 4.81% p.a. provision.
column5: Separate employer obligation from WeCare — shown for completeness

column3: Case specification: ?1,50,00,000 — a 2BHK in a Tier-1 city suburb (Mumbai/Bengaluru/NCR mid-range).
column5: Higher price ? larger loan ? higher EMI ? more stress on take-home & retirement contributions

column1: Down Payment as % of House Price
column3: Baseline assumption: 20% down payment — industry-standard minimum for home loans in India.
column5: Higher down payment ? lower loan ? lower EMI ? more money for retirement savings

column3: Case specification: 7.5% p.a. — reflects current SBI/HDFC floating rates for salaried borrowers (2024-25).
column5: Even a 1% rate rise increases EMI by ??7,000 on a ?1.2Cr loan

column3: Case specification: 20 years — the standard tenure for salaried borrowers.
column5: EMI formula: P × r(1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n ? 1), r = monthly rate, n = months

column3: Industry rule of thumb: EMI should not exceed 35% of net monthly income; banks' FOIR ceiling can be up to 50%.
column5: Used to compute the maximum safe EMI and maximum affordable loan for each profile
